passages.explanation: Paul expresses great sorrow and unceasing anguish in his heart. He could wish himself cursed and cut off from Christ for the sake of his people, the Israelites. Theirs is the adoption to sonship, the divine glory, the covenants, the receiving of the law, the temple worship, the promises, the patriarchs, and from them is traced the human ancestry of the Messiah, who is God over all, forever praised. After the triumph of chapter 8, Paul's tone shifts dramatically. He's heartbroken over Israel's rejection of Jesus. The list of Israel's privileges is staggering — and makes their unbelief all the more tragic. Paul would trade his own salvation for theirs. This begins a three-chapter section (9-11) addressing the hardest question his theology raises: if salvation is by faith, what about God's chosen people who haven't believed?

It's not as though God's word has failed. For not all who are descended from Israel are Israel. Nor because they are his descendants are they all Abraham's children — on the contrary, it is through Isaac that your offspring will be reckoned. In other words, it's not the children by physical descent who are God's children, but the children of the promise who are regarded as Abraham's offspring. Paul traces the pattern: God chose Isaac over Ishmael, Jacob over Esau — before either was born or had done anything good or bad, so that God's purpose in election might stand. It depends not on human desire or effort but on God's mercy. Scripture says to Pharaoh: I raised you up for this very purpose, that I might display my power in you. Therefore God has mercy on whom he wants to have mercy, and he hardens whom he wants to harden. Paul argues that God's election has never been automatic or ethnic. From the beginning, God chose within Abraham's line — not everyone descended from Abraham is part of the promise. This isn't a new development; it's how God has always worked.

Someone will say: then why does God still blame us? Who is able to resist his will? But who are you, a human being, to talk back to God? Shall what is formed say to the one who formed it, why did you make me like this? Does not the potter have the right to make out of the same lump of clay some pottery for special purposes and some for common use? What if God, although choosing to show his wrath and make his power known, bore with great patience the objects of his wrath — prepared for destruction? What if he did this to make the riches of his glory known to the objects of his mercy, whom he prepared in advance for glory — even us, whom he also called, not only from the Jews but also from the Gentiles? As Hosea says: I will call them 'my people' who are not my people. And Isaiah cries out: though the number of the Israelites be like the sand by the sea, only the remnant will be saved. Paul uses the potter-and-clay image from Isaiah and Jeremiah. The point isn't arbitrary cruelty but sovereign freedom — God has the right to shape his purposes as he sees fit. And his current purpose includes calling Gentiles as his people and saving a remnant of Israel. Both were prophesied.

What then shall we say? That the Gentiles, who did not pursue righteousness, have obtained it — a righteousness that is by faith. But the people of Israel, who pursued the law as the way of righteousness, have not attained their goal. Why not? Because they pursued it not by faith but as if it were by works. They stumbled over the stumbling stone. As it is written: see, I lay in Zion a stone that causes people to stumble and a rock that makes them fall, and the one who believes in him will never be put to shame. The irony is sharp: those who weren't trying (Gentiles) found righteousness through faith, while those who were trying hard (Israel) missed it because they pursued it through works instead of faith. Jesus is the stumbling stone — you either build on him or trip over him.
